title: Partial solvability induced by dark states in a box trap with decentered two-body interaction
authors: Hossein Abedi, Nathan L. Harshman, Peter Schmelcher
abstract: We consider a generalization of the two-body contact interaction for nonrelativistic particles confined to a one-dimensional box, in which the interaction is decentered, i.e., the particles interact only when they are separated by a distance c. In contrast to the harmonically trapped system, this model is nonintegrable. Despite this, we demonstrate that the system exhibits partial solvability due to the presence of dark states, i.e., bosonic or fermionic states unaffected by the interaction. These states form exactly solvable subspaces embedded within an interacting spectrum. We characterize the stationary properties of the system, identify the conditions for the appearance of dark states, and show how they structure the spectrum and delineate interacting and noninteracting sectors.
summary: This paper explores a two-particle system in a 1D box where the interaction is shifted by a distance c. While the system is generally non-integrable, the authors find that for certain rational values of c, 'dark states' emerge that are completely unaffected by the interaction strength. These states create exactly solvable sectors within the spectrum. The work bridges the gap between integrable contact interactions and complex, non-integrable dynamics.
main_problem: The paper investigates the emergence of partial solvability and 'dark states' in a non-integrable quantum system where a two-body interaction is displaced from the center.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that specific rational values of the displacement parameter c induce exactly solvable subspaces (dark states) within an otherwise non-integrable spectrum, where wavefunctions are insensitive to interaction strength.
method: The study employs exact diagonalization (ED) using the Lanczos algorithm, compares results with the Bethe ansatz for the c=0 limit, and uses quantum billiards theory to analyze the strong interaction limit.
model_or_system: A system of two non-relativistic particles (bosons or fermions) confined to a one-dimensional infinite square well trap with a decentered, zero-range contact interaction defined by strength g and displacement c.
key_observables: Eigenenergies, wavefunctions, parity, and the presence of spectral degeneracies.
important_parameters: Interaction strength (g), displacement parameter (c), particle statistics (bosonic vs. fermionic), and spatial parity.
main_assumptions: The particles are non-relativistic and the system is restricted to a one-dimensional geometry.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the configuration space regions; Fig 3 visualizes the lowest energy states; Fig 5 illustrates wavefunctions with nodal lines; Fig 6 shows energy vs. interaction strength for a dark state.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the decentered interaction model, analyzes the symmetry properties, explores the integrable limits (c=0 and g=infinity), identifies the conditions for dark states via nodal alignment, and validates numerical results against analytical benchmarks.
why_interesting: It provides a novel mechanism for partial integrability in non-integrable systems and connects the concept of dark states to quantum many-body scars, which is highly relevant to many-body dynamics and thermalization.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Configuration space for two particles confined in a box trap of length L. The interaction potential has support on the two lines x2 −x1 = ±c (dashed lines), which divides the space into three regions.

FIG. 2. The ground-state wave function ψ(x1, x2) in the bosonic even-parity sector (σ, π) = (+1, +1), for interaction strengths g = 0, 1, 20, 100 (rows, top to bottom) and displacement parameters c = 0, 0.1, 0.5, 0.9 (columns, left to right). The dashed lines indicate the support of the interaction potential. At c = 0, the model reduces to the standard contact interaction.
FIG. 3. The six lowest-lying eigenfunctions Ψnm(x1, x2) on the right isosceles triangular domain of leg length a. Quantum numbers (n, m) are given in the figure.

FIG. 4. Energy levels at g →∞. (a) Bosonic even-parity and fermionic odd-parity sectors. (b) Bosonic odd-parity and fermionic even-parity sectors. εout nb,f denotes the doubly degenerate fermionic and bosonic energy levels of the region outside. εin nb and εin nf denote the non-degenerate levels of the region inside for bosonic and fermionic states, respectively. The exact crossings between the lowest-energy levels εout 0b,f and εin 0b,f define four regimes: (E) exclusion, with only εout nb,f ; (T) truncation, with only εin nb,f ; and two crossover regions (C) where they coexist; red: εout nb with εin nb; green: εout nb,f with both εin nf , εin nb. The triple degeneracy indicated by the...

FIG. 5. Wavefunctions of the lowest-lying g = 0 eigenstates with diagonal nodal lines. The corresponding symmetry sector is shown in the figure. (a) and (d) carry diagonal nodal lines only at c = 0, and are therefore dark at c = 0. (b) and (e) States that become dark at c = 1/2. (c) State that becomes dark at c = 1/3. (f) State that becomes dark at c = 2/3.

FIG. 6. Energy vs. g in the bosonic even parity sector at c = 1/2. The 4th excited state is dark. The 2nd and 3rd excited states approach the energy of the dark state asymptotically, giving rise to a triple degeneracy in the g →∞limit.

FIG. 7. Dark state quantum numbers (n, m) as a function of displacement c = p/q. For each point shown, an exact analytical solution exists. Integer scaling generates an infinite tower at fixed c; only (2, 1) →(4, 2) at c = 1/2 is shown.
